item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

The implication that Apollo 11 was in fact the final success of at least 1096 attempts is powerful.

That said, things don't fall out of orbit in general unless they're dipping into an atmosphere. I do know that close lunar orbit was funky due to mass concentrations in the moon itself, though it and might have required some powered station-keeping.

That and, uh, 'ample' is an odd descriptor in that particular context, I feel.

'Real' science should never get in the way of anomalies, though, so you're encouraged to ignore my complaints on that score.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made me feel feelings, you sonofabitch. I'm editing the audio adaptation now, you've got my upvote, but this one needed me to specially tell you how I feel because you made me feel it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You think consistently writing great articles'll make me forgive you for using furry emoticons in #site19? You're sorely mistaken, Teeth.

Like it! Short and sweet, eerie in its own way, yaddah yaddah. It capitalizes on the scary thing about space travel (and sea/air travel, for that matter), the whole "if one technical malfunction goes unaddressed you're totally helpless and plenty of people might not even know you've died" thing. And you removed a part I said felt tonally inconsistent, which gives me this "my decisions actually matter!" dopamine boost no matter how many times it happens. Booyeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the 'article was written in the future' hints that are thrown in: "civilian space exploration", "machinery developed by NASA between 1969 and 2045". +1

item.title: check dis out
item.content:encoded:
The shortest article I've ever snatched helped to write. Received as a (very nearly done) draft in a draft swap from the awesome MaliceAforethought! Thank you to everyone in 19 (ihp, RockTeethMothEyes, and all my other buddies) who gave crit!

Space sure is spooky.
